content:
The character of the pheasant is also a highly significant symbol. There is a point when Phoenix notices a bird which briefly distracts her. As she looks at the bird, Welty describes, "She stood there with a sense of being under the influence of something which had nothing to do with what she was about." The pheasant symbolizes Phoenix's love for nature, as well as her ongoing conflict between hope and despair. The bird, free to fly, is a symbol of a freedom and release ideal that Phoenix is drawn to but is unable to achieve. Her physical frailty, as represented by her slow, struggling movements, contrasts with the flight of the pheasant, highlighting the difference between Phoenix's life and the freedom that she envisions. Yet she presses on despite her own physical exhaustion, representing the sacrifices that she willingly undertakes for her family's sake.

The worn path and the pheasant are utilized together to establish the theme of sacrifice. Phoenix's trip is not for herself but for her grandson, once more repeating the idea that she will go through whatever difficulties are required to take care of him, even when it is obvious that the journey will be difficult. With these symbols, Welty portrays that endurance does not come cheap, yet it is motivated by an unstoppable commitment and love.
